title: Department of Homeland Security Climate Change Research Act
short_title: Department of Homeland Security Climate Change Research Act
plain_summary: This bill tells the Department of Homeland Security to study how climate change hurts our country's safety. The department must figure out ways to protect us from these dangers that climate change causes. They have to write a report about what they learn and send it to Congress every year for four years. This will help our government understand and prepare for security problems that come from climate change.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Emergency Management and Technology.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Department of Homeland Security Climate Change Research Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Climate Change Research and Development
sections.text:
This section changes an existing law called the Homeland Security Act of two thousand two by adding a new section. The new section creates a climate change research program at the Department of Homeland Security.

The Under Secretary for Science and Technology must study existing government research about how to reduce the harmful effects of climate change on homeland security. This study must look at three main areas. First, it must examine how climate change affects emergency planning and disaster response. Second, it must review whether the government's disaster funding system is properly set up to handle climate change problems. Third, it can look at other related issues the Under Secretary thinks are important.

After completing this study, the Under Secretary may conduct new research to find ways to reduce climate change's harmful effects on homeland security. However, this new research can only happen if Congress provides money for it.

The research program must focus first on climate change problems that hurt the Department of Homeland Security's ability to do its job. The program can also study the same areas mentioned in the evaluation.

When doing this work, the Under Secretary must talk with other government agencies and departments. The Under Secretary must also consult with state, local, tribal, and territorial governments. The Under Secretary may also work with companies that own and operate critical infrastructure like power plants and airports.

For this law, climate change means detectable changes in the climate system. These changes happen because of human activities that alter the gases in the Earth's atmosphere.

The Under Secretary must send a report to Congress about this research work. The first report is due one year after this law takes effect. Then the Under Secretary must send three more reports, one each year for the next three years. These reports go to the House Committee on Homeland Security, the House Committee on Science Space and Technology, and the Senate Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

Finally, this section updates the table of contents in the Homeland Security Act to include the new climate change research section.
